one_line_takeaway: This SoK maps LLM-as-a-Judge security into a five-part taxonomy (attacks on LaaJ, attacks via LaaJ, LaaJ-as-defense, LaaJ meta-evaluation, and security-domain applications) built from 45 papers filtered out of 863, and concludes LaaJ systems are widely vulnerable to training- and inference-time manipulation with no adequate existing defense, in a field that only took off in 2025.
key_finding: Aggregating across the reviewed literature, the SoK concludes that LLM-based judges are broadly and systematically non-robust to adversarial manipulation at both training and inference time, that this weakness generalizes across model families, evaluation formats (pointwise/pairwise/listwise), and deployment contexts, and that no single existing defense is adequate (the reviewed defenses are evaded a substantial fraction of the time). Separately, the survey's own bibliometric analysis shows the field is very young and growing rapidly: only 3 of the 45 selected papers were published in 2024 versus 33 in 2025, with early-2026 output suggesting continued expansion; most work appears in conferences and a notable share is still arXiv preprints, indicating an exploratory, not-yet-standardized research area.
core_contribution: The first Systematization of Knowledge (SoK) on the security of LLM-as-a-Judge (LaaJ) systems. The authors searched Google Scholar, IEEE Xplore, ACM DL, Springer, and ScienceDirect for combinations of 'LLM-as-a-Judge' with 'security', 'privacy', 'toxicity', 'reliability'; screened 863 works down to 45 papers (2020-2026, prioritizing Q1 journals/A*-A conferences, plus a small number of arXiv preprints from authors with >5,000 citations). From this corpus they build a five-perspective taxonomy: (i) attacks targeting LaaJ systems, (ii) attacks performed through LaaJ (LaaJ used as an attack instrument), (iii) defenses that leverage LaaJ as a detection/security-analysis tool, (iv) papers that evaluate LaaJ itself (meta-evaluation), and (v) applications of LaaJ as an evaluation strategy in security-related domains (e.g. software engineering/code review, smart environments, CTI). The paper also contributes a comparative synthesis (Table 3, per-paper scope/taxonomy/context), a bibliometric trend analysis (publication counts by year and venue type), a set of orthogonal binary design dimensions for classifying LaaJ systems (model access, scoring method, deployment, subject of evaluation, rubric design, knowledge source, usage stage), and an open-problems agenda for three categories of judge weaknesses: adversarial prompt manipulation, positional bias, and length/style bias.
threat_model: The survey does not itself instantiate an attacker; it catalogs and organizes the threat models used across the 45 reviewed studies. Two temporal classes are distinguished for attacks targeting LaaJ: (1) training-time attacks, where the adversary poisons the judge's fine-tuning data/labels (backdoor/poisoning) or manipulates natural-language evaluation rubrics without any model or data access (rubric/protocol manipulation); and (2) inference-time attacks, where the adversary crafts adversarial candidate responses or system prompts at evaluation time (prompt injection, token/surface-level perturbation such as emoji or delimiter insertion, and broader robustness-benchmark attacks). A second, orthogonal category covers LaaJ as an attack instrument (LLM judges themselves exploited to conduct or launder malicious activity) rather than as the attack target. The paper frames adversaries as ranging from black-box actors with only text-level access to full weight-poisoning insiders, consistent with the range of threat models used by the individual cited studies (e.g. BadJudge's web-poisoning/malicious-annotation/weight-poisoning tiers).
limitations: As a SoK/survey the paper has no threats-to-validity from its own experiments, but the authors' stated scope limits apply: reliance on database/keyword search (Google Scholar, IEEE Xplore, ACM DL, Springer, ScienceDirect) with a fixed keyword set, which can miss work using different terminology; exclusion of non-English publications and of prior surveys/literature reviews from the corpus; inclusion of a 'limited number' of preprints gated only by an author-citation-count heuristic (>5,000 citations) rather than peer review, introducing a selection bias toward already-prominent authors; and a corpus (45 papers) that the authors themselves note leaves standardized benchmarks and robust, agreed-upon evaluation methodologies for LaaJ security still lacking across the field.

datasets_used: N/A - survey/SoK: no original dataset was used; the 'corpus' analyzed is 45 selected papers drawn from 863 candidates found via Google Scholar, IEEE Xplore, ACM Digital Library, Springer, and ScienceDirect (2020-2026)
quantitative_results: SoK's own bibliometrics: 863 candidate works screened down to 45 included studies (2020-2026)
SoK's own bibliometrics: publication trend for LaaJ-security work rose from 3 papers in 2024 to 33 papers in 2025, with continued growth into early 2026 (survey's Figure 7)

Cited study BadJudge (Tong et al. [50]): poisoning as little as 1% of judge training data triples the adversary's score and yields an attack success rate over 76%; web-poisoning alone induces 20% score inflation; full weight-poisoning inflates adversary scores from 1.5/5 to 4.9/5; backdoored guardrails misclassify toxic content as safe 83.9% of the time; backdoored RAG rerankers surface poisoned documents as top result 96.9% of the time

Cited study on Rubric-Induced Preference Drift, RIPD (Ding et al. [9]): benchmark-compliant rubric edits reduce judge accuracy by up to 9.5% on helpfulness tasks and 27.9% on harmlessness tasks, transferring across Qwen3-14B, Gemma-3-27B-it, and DeepSeek-V3

Cited study Raina et al. [38]: four-word universal adversarial phrases (found via greedy search on a FlanT5-3B surrogate) transfer to Llama2-7B, Mistral-7B, and GPT-3.5 to force maximum scores; a perplexity-based defense achieves F1 of 0.70-0.82

Cited study Maloyan and Namiot [31]: system-prompt injection attacks outperform content-author attacks by 15 percentage points on average; Adaptive Search-Based Attack reaches up to 73.8% success against Gemma-3-4B-Instruct; open-source judges show 50-68% average attack success vs. 27-44% for GPT-4/Claude-3-Opus; individual defenses are evaded 32-67% of the time, while a seven-model committee reduces attack success to 10-19%

Cited study JudgeDeceiver (Shi et al. [44]): optimization-based prompt injection achieves 89-99% average attack success rate across four open-source LLMs and two benchmarks (MT-Bench, LLMBar), versus ~40% for the best manual baseline; attacks optimized on Llama-3-8B transfer with 88% success to Claude-3 Sonnet and 79% to GPT-4; three detection defenses (known-answer, perplexity, windowed perplexity) show false-negative rates of 40-100%

Cited study Emoji Attack (Wei et al. [55]): across ten judge LLMs (Llama Guard, WildGuard, ShieldLM, GPT-4, Claude, etc.) reduces unsafe-content detection by an average of 12%; ShieldLM's detection rate drops from 71.9% to 3.5% specifically under the Deepinception jailbreak

Cited study Zhao et al. [64] (reward-model 'master keys'): trivial inputs such as punctuation marks or reasoning-opener phrases elicit false positive rewards in 80-90% of cases across models including GPT-4o, GPT-o1, Claude-4, and Qwen2.5-72B-Instruct
Cited study Li et al. [28]: systematic robustness benchmark across 15 attack methods and 12 models finds prompt-template choice alone can shift robustness by up to 40 percentage points
baselines_compared: N/A - this is a survey with no experimental baselines of its own; it instead positions itself against prior LLM-as-a-Judge surveys, distinguishing itself as the first to focus specifically on security: contrasted with a general reliability-focused LaaJ survey [14], a broader functionality/methodology/applications survey that only briefly touches adversarial attacks in its limitations [26], a taxonomy survey organized around what/how/how-to-benchmark that treats security as one evaluation attribute among others [23], and domain-specific surveys in software engineering [15] and healthcare [12, 20] that mention adversarial threats only in passing
